content.text: Vendor and partner governance is the structured framework through which an organisation manages its relationships with AI platform vendors, model providers, system integrators, and data partners across the full delivery lifecycle — from vendor selection and contracting through operational performance management, risk oversight, and exit planning.

An enterprise AI programme typically depends on multiple external parties: model API providers (Anthropic, OpenAI, Cohere), cloud AI platforms (Azure AI, AWS Bedrock, Google Vertex AI), system integrators (Accenture, Deloitte, Infosys) who deliver implementation work, and data vendors who supply training or enrichment data. Each of these relationships introduces a distinct risk category and requires a distinct governance posture.

Vendor governance operates across three layers. Contractual governance covers the legal and compliance instruments: the master services agreement (MSA), data processing agreement (DPA), business associate agreement (BAA) for HIPAA-covered data, SOC 2 Type II certification requirements, SLA definitions with penalty clauses, pricing schedules, and exit clauses that specify data portability and transition assistance obligations. Operational governance covers the ongoing performance management: quarterly business reviews (QBRs), monthly SLA tracking, incident escalation protocols, and change notification requirements. Strategic governance covers portfolio-level vendor risk: concentration risk (reliance on a single provider for a critical capability), geopolitical risk for offshore data processing, financial stability assessment, and multi-vendor strategy to maintain competitive alternatives.

AI programmes have a higher degree of vendor dependency than traditional software projects because the core capability — LLM inference, embedding models, vector databases — is typically provided as a service rather than built in-house. This means vendor failures, pricing changes, capability deprecations, and regulatory non-compliance at the vendor affect the AI programme directly and often with short notice.

A bank managing 7 AI vendors without a formal governance framework discovered two SLA breaches that had been ongoing for weeks — one in model API availability, one in data delivery timing — only when downstream business metrics deteriorated. After implementing monthly SLA tracking with automated alerting, the same bank caught 2 SLA breaches within the first quarter, triggering contractual penalty clauses that recovered $1.2M in service credits and prompted both vendors to address underlying infrastructure issues. Early detection was only possible because SLA thresholds were configured in a monitoring system rather than left to manual review.
Structured vendor risk registers surface concentration risk, geopolitical exposure, and financial stability concerns before they become incidents.
Properly negotiated DPAs, BAAs, and exit clauses protect the organisation's data rights and transition options.
QBRs with published KPIs create accountability structures that prevent vendor SLA degradation going unaddressed.
Regular pricing reviews catch model behind token-cost increases and trigger renegotiation while the organisation still has leverage.
SOC 2 Type II requirements and DPA controls give compliance and legal teams the documentation needed for regulatory audits.
Maintaining vendor scorecards, scheduling QBRs, and tracking SLAs across 7+ vendors requires a dedicated programme management resource or a GRC tool.
Large model providers have standard contracts; smaller enterprises have limited power to negotiate custom terms. Governance must work within available contract structures.
Automated SLA monitoring requires integration with vendor status pages and internal observability systems — an upfront implementation investment.
A multi-vendor strategy reduces lock-in but increases integration complexity, credential management burden, and the number of SLAs to track.

Vendor governance begins at vendor selection, not after contract signature. Selection criteria should be evaluated and documented before any vendor is selected: security posture (SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001), SLA commitments and historical uptime, pricing model and cost predictability, data handling policies (data retention, use of customer data for model training, residency requirements), and exit clauses (data portability, transition assistance periods, minimum notice for capability deprecation).

Once a vendor is onboarded, the operational governance cadence activates. Monthly: pull automated SLA metrics from the monitoring system and compare against contractual thresholds. If a threshold is breached, initiate the SLA breach escalation path — first notification to the vendor account manager, escalation to the vendor executive sponsor if not resolved within the vendor's SLA. Quarterly: conduct the QBR. The QBR agenda covers: KPI performance vs targets, upcoming roadmap changes, pricing review, risk register update, and action item tracking from the previous QBR.

Annually: the strategic governance review. This covers contract renewal, exit clause evaluation (is the notice period still adequate given current integration depth?), concentration risk assessment (what percentage of critical AI workload is on this vendor?), and financial stability check (annual reports, credit ratings, funding news for smaller vendors).

attrs.alt: Vendor Governance Layers: Strategic (annual, CPO/CTO/Legal), Operational (quarterly QBRs and monthly SLA tracking, PM/Vendor AM/Tech Lead), Contractual (MSA/DPA/BAA/SOC2, Legal/Procurement/Compliance). Five vendor categories shown at bottom: Model API Providers, Cloud AI Platforms, System Integrators, Data Vendors, Tooling/Infra.

content.text: The three-layer architecture prevents the common failure mode where vendor management lives entirely in procurement (contractual layer only) with no operational performance tracking, or where programme teams track performance without legal or compliance awareness of the contractual protections in place. All three layers must be active and connected.
Any AI programme that depends on external model API calls for core functionality — the provider's availability directly affects the programme's production reliability.
Programmes with more than 3 active AI vendors, where informal tracking is insufficient to maintain visibility across all relationships.
Programmes where vendor data processing involves personal data subject to GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA, or other data protection regulation — contractual DPA and BAA controls are legally required, not optional.
Programmes in regulated industries (financial services, healthcare, energy) where vendor third-party risk management is required by the regulator (FCA, OCC, CMS).
Any programme where a single vendor provides more than 40% of a critical AI capability — concentration risk governance is required at this threshold.

Start with a vendor registry — a structured configuration that captures each vendor's contract status, SLA definitions, alerting thresholds, and review cadence. The YAML below shows a vendor registry entry for a model API provider and a cloud AI platform. Store this file in the programme's governance repository and treat it as the source of truth for vendor SLA monitoring configuration.

# vendor-config.yaml  —  vendor registry with SLA thresholds and alerting
vendors:
  - id: "anthropic-claude"
    name: "Anthropic Claude API"
    category: model_api_provider
    tier: critical                     # critical | important | standard
    primary_use: "LLM inference for customer-facing summarisation"
    contract:
      type: enterprise_api             # enterprise_api | cloud_platform | SI | data | tooling
      msa_signed: true
      dpa_signed: true
      baa_required: false
      soc2_type2: true
      last_security_review: "2026-01-15"
      next_review_due: "2027-01-15"
      exit_notice_days: 90
      data_residency: "EU"
      pricing_model: token_based
    slas:
      api_availability: 99.9            # % uptime commitment
      p99_latency_ms: 3000
      incident_response_hours: 4        # vendor response SLA on Sev-1
      incident_resolution_hours: 24
    alerting:
      availability_threshold_pct: 99.5  # alert when below this
      latency_p99_ms: 4000              # alert when above this
      alert_channel: "#ai-vendor-alerts"
    risk:
      concentration_risk: high          # high: >40% AI workload on single vendor
      geo_political_risk: low
      financial_stability: high         # public company / well-capitalised
    review_cadence:
      qbr_frequency: quarterly
      sla_review_frequency: monthly
      last_qbr: "2026-04-10"
      next_qbr: "2026-07-10"
    kpis:
      - metric: api_availability_pct
        target: 99.9
        last_value: 99.97
        trend: stable
      - metric: p99_latency_ms
        target: 3000
        last_value: 1840
        trend: improving
      - metric: token_cost_per_1k
        target: 0.030
        last_value: 0.028
        trend: stable

  - id: "azure-openai-service"
    name: "Azure OpenAI Service"
    category: cloud_ai_platform
    tier: critical
    primary_use: "Embedding generation and secondary inference fallback"
    contract:
      type: cloud_platform
      msa_signed: true
      dpa_signed: true
      baa_required: true
      soc2_type2: true
      exit_notice_days: 30
      data_residency: "EU"
    slas:
      api_availability: 99.9
      p99_latency_ms: 2500
      incident_response_hours: 1
      incident_resolution_hours: 8
    alerting:
      availability_threshold_pct: 99.5
      latency_p99_ms: 3500
      alert_channel: "#ai-vendor-alerts"
    risk:
      concentration_risk: medium
      geo_political_risk: low
      financial_stability: very_high
    review_cadence:
      qbr_frequency: quarterly
      sla_review_frequency: monthly

Feed the SLA thresholds from this configuration into your monitoring stack. For cloud AI platforms, subscribe to the vendor's status page RSS feed or use a third-party uptime monitoring service (Better Uptime, StatusPage) configured against the `availability_threshold_pct` values. For SI delivery quality, define KPIs in the project tracking tool (on-time delivery rate, defect escape rate) and include them in the QBR scorecard.

A financial services firm discovered during its annual vendor review that 68% of its AI inference workload ran on a single model API provider. The concentration risk threshold was 40%. The strategic governance review resulted in a 6-month project to onboard a secondary provider with a 30% traffic split, reducing concentration to 46% — still above target but mitigated. The action was recorded in the vendor risk register with a 12-month target to reach 40%.

A healthcare technology company found during a monthly SLA review that its data vendor had missed the contracted delivery time for weekly claims enrichment data 3 times in the past quarter. The programme manager initiated the SLA breach escalation, the vendor account team acknowledged the breach, and a service credit was applied per the contract terms. The root cause (an underpowered ETL pipeline at the vendor) was identified and remediated. Without monthly tracking, the repeated breach would have been invisible until the downstream impact appeared in model quality metrics.

A tier-1 bank managed 7 AI vendors across its retail and commercial banking AI programme: two model API providers, one cloud AI platform (Azure OpenAI Service), two data vendors, one SI for implementation, and one observability tooling vendor. The bank established a vendor governance office within the AI programme management function, staffed by one dedicated vendor governance manager.

The governance manager implemented monthly SLA tracking using automated dashboard pulls from the vendors' status pages and the bank's own API gateway metrics (actual latency and availability as observed from the bank's infrastructure, not vendor-reported). In the first quarter of operation, the dashboard identified an API availability breach by one model provider (99.7% vs the contracted 99.9%) that had not been reported by the vendor. The bank raised the breach, received a service credit of £85,000 against the annual contract, and the vendor committed to an infrastructure upgrade. A second breach — data delivery latency from a data vendor — triggered a contract review that resulted in a penalty clause amendment and a 15% price reduction in exchange for waiving one month of penalties. Total savings from early breach detection in year 1: $1.2M.
Signing a DPA without reviewing data training clauses:
Some model providers' standard DPAs allow them to use customer-provided data to improve their models. For regulated industries, this is frequently prohibited and must be explicitly excluded.
No exit clause or inadequate notice period:
A 30-day exit notice may be insufficient if the vendor provides a capability that takes 6 months to replace. Exit clause terms must reflect actual migration timelines.
A model API provider supplying 60% of AI inference requires Critical tier governance; a supplementary tooling vendor requires Standard tier. Applying the same QBR cadence to both wastes effort.
Relying on vendor self-reported SLA metrics:
Vendors measure availability from their infrastructure boundary; the bank's observed availability includes network latency and API gateway variability. Always measure from the consumer's perspective.
Teams make vendor choices incrementally without realising that, in aggregate, they have placed excessive reliance on a single provider.

Establish a vendor tier system (Critical, Important, Standard) and apply proportionate governance to each tier — monthly SLA reviews and quarterly QBRs for Critical, quarterly SLA review for Standard.
Negotiate data processing agreements before the first API call, not after the project is in production. Retroactive DPA negotiation is significantly harder.
Require SOC 2 Type II reports annually from all Critical-tier vendors. Do not accept SOC 2 Type I — Type I only audits control design, not operational effectiveness.

Define exit criteria and transition assistance requirements in the initial contract. Exit clauses should specify: minimum notice period, data export format, whether the vendor must provide reasonable technical assistance during transition, and whether the vendor can continue operating the service under existing terms during the exit period.

Run a concentration risk assessment quarterly across the full vendor portfolio. Include both financial concentration (percentage of AI budget) and capability concentration (percentage of critical workflow depending on a single provider).

Include vendor risk as a standing agenda item at the steering committee — not just at the annual strategic review. Significant vendor events (pricing changes, capability deprecations, security incidents) warrant in-cycle steering committee attention.

At fewer than 4 AI vendors, a spreadsheet-based vendor scorecard updated monthly by the programme manager is sufficient. Beyond 4 vendors — particularly with multiple Critical-tier vendors — manual tracking becomes error-prone and the overhead of preparation for multiple QBRs accumulates. At this scale, a GRC tool (ServiceNow Vendor Risk Management, Archer, or a dedicated vendor management platform such as Vendr) provides automation for SLA data aggregation, risk scoring, and review scheduling.

Automated SLA monitoring using the vendor configuration YAML (as shown above) can be connected to a Datadog or Grafana dashboard that ingests vendor status page data and internal API gateway metrics. This reduces the monthly SLA review to an exception-based process — the dashboard surfaces breaches automatically and only flagged items require manual investigation.
Enterprise-grade vendor risk management with built-in assessment workflows, SLA tracking, and regulatory questionnaire management.
GRC platform used in financial services for third-party risk management with regulatory mapping to FCA, OCC, and EBA guidelines.
SLA dashboards consuming vendor status page data and internal API gateway metrics to provide consumer-perspective availability tracking.
Vendor governance pages storing QBR records, scorecard history, and DPA status for each vendor — linked from the project governance space.
Vendor SLA breach tickets with defined escalation workflows; QBR action item tracking with owner assignment and due date.
SaaS procurement management platforms that track contract renewal dates, SLA commitments, and spend — useful for smaller teams without a dedicated GRC tool.

Vendor security posture must be assessed at onboarding and reassessed annually. The minimum acceptable evidence set for Critical-tier vendors is: SOC 2 Type II report (issued within the last 12 months), ISO 27001 certificate (if applicable to the service), penetration test summary (executed by an independent third party within the last 12 months), and completion of the programme's vendor security questionnaire. For model API providers, additionally verify: data retention policy (how long are prompts and completions retained?), whether the vendor uses customer data for model training (and confirm this is prohibited in the DPA if required), and the vendor's incident notification obligations and timeline.

Reliability governance requires contractual SLA definitions that specify: measurement methodology (vendor-side vs consumer-side), the time window for SLA calculation (monthly, quarterly), the credit mechanism for SLA breaches (percentage of monthly fee), and the maximum credit cap per period. SLA credits alone do not compensate for the business impact of availability failures — they are a governance signal and a financial incentive for the vendor, not a complete remediation mechanism.

Think of your AI vendor portfolio as a supply chain. Just as a manufacturer tracks supplier quality, delivery reliability, financial stability, and single-source dependency risk across its component suppliers, an AI programme must track the same dimensions for its AI capability suppliers. A supply chain manager would not let 68% of a critical component come from a single supplier without an explicit risk acceptance and mitigation plan — the same discipline applies to AI vendor concentration.

The three governance layers correspond to three time horizons: contractual (multi-year, defines the rules), operational (monthly/quarterly, tracks performance against the rules), and strategic (annual, evaluates whether the rules and the vendor relationships still fit the business direction). All three must be active — contracts without performance tracking are unenforceable in practice; performance tracking without contracts provides no legal protection; strategic review without operational data has no factual grounding.

Vendor governance operates across three layers: contractual (MSA, DPA, BAA, exit clauses), operational (monthly SLA tracking, quarterly QBRs), and strategic (annual concentration risk, multi-vendor strategy).
Five AI vendor categories require distinct governance postures: model API providers, cloud AI platforms, system integrators, data vendors, and tooling/infrastructure.
Negotiate DPAs before the first API call — include explicit prohibition on using customer data for model training and specify data residency requirements.
Measure SLA compliance from the consumer's perspective (your API gateway metrics), not from vendor-reported status pages.
Run concentration risk assessments quarterly — flag any vendor providing more than 40% of a critical AI capability and develop a multi-vendor mitigation plan.
Require SOC 2 Type II (not Type I) annually from Critical-tier vendors. Type II audits operational effectiveness of controls; Type I only audits design.
A bank managing 7 AI vendors caught 2 SLA breaches early through monthly tracking, recovering $1.2M in service credits and preventing extended service degradation.
Exit clauses must specify data export format, transition assistance obligations, and a minimum notice period that reflects actual migration timelines — not the vendor's preferred 30 days.
